section.title: Why Do You Stand Far Off
section.sectionRationale: The second half of the acrostic begun in Psalm 9, an urgent lament over the arrogant wicked who prey on the helpless while God seems distant, resolving in confident trust that the LORD is King forever and hears the desire of the humble.
section.thesis: Though the wicked boast, deny God, and prey on the helpless as if He does not see, David appeals to the LORD, who does see trouble and grief, to rise and act as King forever, breaking the arm of the wicked and vindicating the fatherless and oppressed.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the opening cry over God's apparent distance (vv1-2), a detailed portrait of the wicked's arrogance and predation on the helpless (vv3-11), a plea for God to arise and remember (vv12-15), and the closing confession of the LORD's eternal kingship and care for the oppressed (vv16-18).

units.label: Why do You stand far off
units.note: why do You hide in times of trouble

verses.text: Why, O LORD, do You stand far off? Why do You hide in times of trouble?
In pride the wicked pursue the needy; let them be caught in the schemes they devise.
For the wicked man boasts in the cravings of his heart; he blesses the greedy and reviles the LORD.
In his pride the wicked man does not seek Him; in all his schemes there is no God.
He is secure in his ways at all times; Your lofty judgments are far from him; he sneers at all his foes.
He says to himself, "I will not be moved; from age to age I am free of distress."
His mouth is full of cursing, deceit, and violence; trouble and malice are under his tongue.
He lies in wait near the villages; in ambush he slays the innocent; his eyes watch in stealth for the helpless.
He lies in wait like a lion in a thicket; he lurks to seize the oppressed; he catches the lowly in his net.
They are crushed and beaten down; the hapless fall prey to his strength.
He says to himself, "God has forgotten; He hides His face and never sees."

units.label: Arise, O Lord
units.note: You have regarded trouble and grief

verses.text: Arise, O LORD! Lift up Your hand, O God! Do not forget the helpless.
Why has the wicked man renounced God? He says to himself, "You will never call me to account."
But You have regarded trouble and grief; You see to repay it by Your hand. The victim entrusts himself to You; You are the helper of the fatherless.
Break the arm of the wicked and evildoer; call him to account for his wickedness until none is left to be found.

units.label: The Lord is King forever
units.note: to vindicate the fatherless and oppressed

verses.text: The LORD is King forever and ever; the nations perish from His land.
You have heard, O LORD, the desire of the {{anavim|humble}}; You will strengthen their hearts. You will incline Your ear,
to vindicate the fatherless and oppressed, that the men of the earth may strike terror no more.

anavim.gloss: The humble, the afflicted or lowly, those bent low by hardship who look to God rather than their own strength. The LORD's ear is inclined precisely to this desire, forming a pattern across the Psalms where God favors the humble over the proud, later echoed in the Beatitudes, Matthew 5:5.

basic.context: Psalm 10 continues the acrostic begun in Psalm 9, but its tone shifts sharply to lament. David cries out that God seems to stand far off while the arrogant wicked prey on the helpless and deny that God even sees. The psalm resolves in confidence that the LORD is King forever, who hears the desire of the humble and vindicates the fatherless.
basic.hermeneutics: This psalm gives voice to the painful gap between God's promised justice and its apparent delay, refusing to pretend the wicked's success is not troubling; it teaches that naming this tension honestly to God, rather than suppressing it, is itself an act of faith, since the psalm ends in confident trust rather than despair.
basic.originalLanguages: The wicked man's inner monologue, God has forgotten, He hides His face and never sees, is quoted twice, a rhetorical device exposing the practical atheism behind arrogant oppression. By contrast, the LORD hears the desire of the anavim, the humble or afflicted, those who have nothing to rely on but Him.
basic.typology: David's plea that God rise, break the arm of the wicked, and vindicate the fatherless anticipates the final judgment Christ will bring, when every arrogant claim that God does not see will be answered in full.
basic.christ: Jesus is God's answer to the cry 'why do You stand far off'; in the incarnation God draws near to exactly the helpless and fatherless this psalm describes, and at His return He will fully break the power of every arrogant evildoer described here.
basic.god: God appears distant to the sufferer's experience, yet the psalm insists He does in fact regard trouble and grief and will repay it by His hand, holding together honest complaint about felt divine absence with unwavering confidence in God's actual attentiveness and coming justice.
basic.crossReferences: James 1:27 defines pure religion as caring for orphans and widows in their affliction, echoing this psalm's concern for the fatherless. Matthew 5:5 blesses the meek, echoing God's care for the anavim. Psalm 34:18 promises the LORD is near the brokenhearted.
basic.oneStory: Psalm 10 gives every believer permission to ask honestly why God seems distant while evil flourishes, and answers that question not with an explanation but with a person: the King who reigns forever, who draws near to the helpless in Jesus Christ and will finally silence every arrogant denial that He sees.

context.literary: The second half of an alphabetic acrostic begun in Psalm 9, though several Hebrew letters are missing or irregular in the surviving text, moving from a jarring shift in tone, thanksgiving in Psalm 9 to lament here, through an extended, almost forensic description of the wicked, to a closing confession of trust.
context.historical: Unlike most psalms, Psalm 10 lacks its own superscription, likely because it was originally joined to Psalm 9 as a single composition, a pairing reflected in the Greek Septuagint's numbering of the two as one psalm.
context.canonical: Completes the acrostic pattern begun in Psalm 9, together forming a sustained meditation on God's justice that holds thanksgiving for what God has done together with honest lament over what remains unresolved, a pairing later echoed in many other psalms of complaint and trust.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To voice, without minimizing it, the painful experience of God's apparent distance while the arrogant wicked prey on the helpless, and to move the worshipper from that honest complaint to renewed confidence in God's eternal kingship and coming justice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes a specific, painful experience of injustice and God's apparent silence; it prescribes honest lament rather than either denial of the pain or loss of trust, modeling a pattern of complaint that ends in worship rather than despair.

debates.question: Does asking 'why do You stand far off' represent a lapse in David's faith?

views.label: An honest, faith-filled complaint, not a lapse
views.case: The Psalms repeatedly model this kind of direct questioning as legitimate prayer, and this very psalm ends in confident trust, showing the complaint was a step toward, not away from, deeper faith.

views.label: A description of genuine spiritual struggle that the psalm itself resolves
views.case: The sharp tonal shift from Psalm 9's confidence to this psalm's anguish suggests real fluctuation in the psalmist's felt experience of God, which Scripture includes without condemning, ultimately resolved by remembering God's kingship rather than by denying the struggle occurred.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms that voicing the felt distance of God is legitimate, Spirit-shaped prayer, not evidence of unbelief, especially when, as here, it moves toward renewed trust rather than settling into despair.

study.originalLanguages: The wicked man's arrogance is expressed in direct quotation, twice: God has forgotten, He hides His face and never sees, a rhetorical technique exposing his functional atheism despite outward religiosity. Against this, the LORD hears the desire of the anavim, the humble or afflicted, a term describing those bent low by hardship who have nowhere to turn but to God, a group the Beatitudes later specifically bless, Matthew 5:5.

typology.type: The LORD who hears the desire of the humble and vindicates the fatherless, Psalm 10:17-18
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who drew near to the poor and afflicted in His earthly ministry and will fully vindicate the oppressed and silence all arrogant evil at His return.

study.christ: This psalm's cry, why do You stand far off, finds its answer in the incarnation: God did not remain distant but drew near, in Jesus, to exactly the helpless, fatherless, and oppressed this psalm describes, Luke 4:18. Christ's earthly ministry to the poor and afflicted, and His promised return to judge with perfect justice, is the full answer to David's plea that God arise and break the power of the arrogant wicked forever.
study.god: God appears, in the sufferer's felt experience, to stand far off and hide, yet the psalm insists this is not the truth of the matter: He does regard trouble and grief, He does hear the desire of the humble, and His eternal kingship guarantees that no arrogant evildoer escapes accountability forever.

crossReferences.note: Defines pure and undefiled religion as caring for orphans and widows in their affliction, echoing this psalm's concern for the fatherless.
Blesses the meek, echoing this psalm's confidence that the LORD hears the desire of the anavim, the humble.
Promises that the LORD is near to the brokenhearted, directly answering this psalm's complaint that God seems far off.

study.oneStory: Psalm 10 gives voice to the painful gap between God's promised justice and its felt delay, and resolves that gap not with a mere explanation but with worship of the eternal King who hears the humble and will vindicate the oppressed. Jesus Christ is God's decisive answer, drawing near to the helpless now and guaranteeing final justice at His return.

quiz.q: What is David's opening complaint in Psalm 10:1?

options.text: That God seems to stand far off and hide Himself in times of trouble.
options.note: This is the psalm's direct opening question and complaint.

options.text: That his enemies have already been defeated.
options.note: The complaint is the opposite; the wicked are actively prospering while God seems absent.

options.text: That he has lost his kingdom.
options.note: The complaint concerns God's felt distance, not a specific loss of political power.

options.text: Nothing; the psalm opens in confident praise.
options.note: Verse 1 is an anguished question, not confident praise; that shift happens later in the psalm.

quiz.q: What does the wicked man say to himself, according to verses 6 and 11?

options.text: That he will never be moved and that God has forgotten and does not see.
options.note: This double quotation exposes the wicked man's arrogant, practical denial of God's justice and attention.

options.text: That he fears God's coming judgment.
options.note: The wicked man's inner speech expresses the opposite, confident denial that judgment will ever come.

options.text: That he plans to repent soon.
options.note: No such intention is described; his inner speech is arrogant self-assurance, not repentance.

options.text: Nothing is quoted from the wicked man's thoughts.
options.note: Verses 6 and 11 both directly quote the wicked man's inner thoughts.

quiz.q: How does the psalm describe the wicked man's treatment of the helpless in verses 8-10?

options.text: He lies in wait like a lion, ambushing and preying on the innocent, oppressed, and lowly.
options.note: This vivid predatory imagery describes the wicked man's violence against the vulnerable.

options.text: He generously provides for the poor.
options.note: This is the opposite of the psalm's description, which portrays predatory violence against the helpless.

options.text: He ignores the poor entirely, neither helping nor harming them.
options.note: The passage describes active predation and ambush, not mere indifference.

options.text: The psalm gives no description of his actions.
options.note: Verses 8-10 give a detailed, vivid description of his predatory behavior.

quiz.q: What does David affirm about God in verse 14, despite the earlier complaint?

options.text: That God has in fact regarded trouble and grief and will repay it by His hand; He is the helper of the fatherless.
options.note: This is the psalm's turning point, affirming God's actual attentiveness despite how distant He may feel.

options.text: That God truly has forgotten the oppressed forever.
options.note: This is precisely what the psalm rejects; verse 14 affirms God has in fact regarded trouble and grief.

options.text: That justice will only come in the afterlife, never in history.
options.note: The psalm does not specify only an afterlife resolution; it affirms God's active attentiveness and coming action generally.

options.text: Nothing about God's attentiveness is affirmed here.
options.note: Verse 14 makes a direct, positive affirmation about God regarding trouble and grief.

quiz.q: How does the psalm close in verses 16-18?

options.text: With confidence that the LORD is King forever, who hears the humble and vindicates the fatherless and oppressed.
options.note: This is the psalm's confident resolution after the earlier lament.

options.text: In continued despair with no resolution.
options.note: The psalm moves to confident affirmation of God's kingship and justice by its close, not unresolved despair.

options.text: With a call for Israel to take up arms against the nations.
options.note: The closing confession is about God's eternal kingship and care for the oppressed, not a military call to arms.

options.text: The psalm has no closing section.
options.note: Verses 16-18 form a clear, deliberate closing confession of trust.

questions: David honestly asks God why He seems far off while evil prospers. Is there a situation in your life right now where you feel God is distant, and what would it look like to bring that question honestly to Him rather than suppress it?

The wicked man in this psalm convinces himself that God does not see and will never call him to account. Where might you be tempted to act as though God is not watching, even while maintaining outward religious appearances?
The psalm describes real predatory harm done to the helpless and vulnerable. Who around you is currently vulnerable or being taken advantage of, and how might God be calling you to stand with them?
Despite the earlier complaint, David affirms that God has in fact regarded trouble and grief. What past evidence of God's attentiveness can you hold onto when He currently feels distant?

The psalm ends with confidence in God's eternal kingship rather than an explanation of why evil was allowed to continue. How does trusting God's ultimate reign, rather than needing every question answered now, change the way you wait through unresolved injustice?

translationNotes.note: This psalm carries no title of its own, likely because it originally formed one composition with Psalm 9 as part of a continuous alphabetic acrostic, a unity the Greek Septuagint preserves by numbering the two together as a single psalm.
He hides His face and never sees quotes the wicked man's own cynical reasoning, not a statement of fact; the psalm's later verses directly refute this claim about God.
The humble renders anavim, a term for those bent low by affliction or oppression who depend wholly on God, a recurring category of person across the Psalms whom God specifically favors and hears.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Psalm 10:1-18. No em or en dashes appear in this psalm's BSB text, and none are used anywhere in this file. No sermon from the trusted cluster could be confirmed this session for Psalm 10, so sermon status is none.
